metadata.title: Why Unknown Doesn't Mean Avoid: The Name-Match Gap
metadata.note: Exclusions: no Levenshtein/fuzzy matching algorithms; no full entity-resolution system design; no legal definition of a legal entity. One mechanism only: punctuation-blind join fragments one company into separate buckets; coverage audit is the diagnostic. Worked example uses NovaGen (illustrative) per card.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.name: Title
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The engine scores a company. No visa sponsorship history. Unknown tier. Move on.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. The engine scores a company. No visa sponsorship history. Unknown tier. Move on.

beats.name: ColdScene
beats.narration_text: But the company has been sponsoring H-1B workers for three years. Both things are true at the same time.

props.headline: But the company has been sponsoring H-1B workers for three years.…

beats.scene_description: a printout of an employer database report pinned to aged newsprint, showing a company row stamped UNKNOWN in large text, while beside it a stack of paper LCA filings spills out of a folder — editorial collage
beats.image_prompt: B02, a printed employer database report pinned to aged newsprint with the word UNKNOWN stamped in bold ink next to a company name, beside it a folder spilling paper LCA government filings, desaturated editorial collage, flat print reproduction, documentary still

build.suggested: suggested prompt: But the company has been sponsoring H-1B workers for three years. Both things are true at the same time.

beats.name: TheQuestion
beats.narration_text: A job engine should know the difference between a company that doesn't sponsor and one it simply cannot find. Here is the case where they look identical. Why?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A job engine should know the difference between a company that doesn't sponsor and one it simply cannot find. Here is the case where they look identical. Why?

beats.name: TwoUnknowns
beats.narration_text: Unknown tier has two very different causes. First: a company with no filing history — they have never sponsored, or they are too young for the data to exist yet.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Unknown tier has two very different causes. First: a company with no filing history — they have never sponsored, or they are too young for the data to exist yet

beats.name: NameMismatchCause
beats.narration_text: Second: a company with a filing history — but the pipeline could not match its name across the two datasets. The filings exist. They just never joined.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Second: a company with a filing history — but the pipeline could not match its name across the two datasets. The filings exist. They just never joined.

beats.name: ThreeBuckets
beats.narration_text: Here is how fragmentation happens. The pipeline joins two government datasets on company name. DOL has LCA filings. USCIS has H-1B approvals. If one company appears under different names in each database, the join creates separate buckets.

props.headline: Here is how fragmentation happens. The pipeline joins two government datasets…

beats.scene_description: an overhead view of three paper file folders labeled with slightly different company name variants, separated on a newsprint surface, editorial collage
beats.image_prompt: B06, three manila file folders spread on aged newsprint, each labeled with a slightly different company name — one in caps, one mixed case, one with LLC — separated from each other, overhead flat-lay editorial collage, desaturated tones

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Here is how fragmentation happens. The pipeline joins two government datasets on company name. DOL has LCA filings. USCIS has H-1B approvals. If one company app

beats.name: BucketDiagram
beats.narration_text: Three name variants — BioTechCo LLC, BIOTECHCO LLC, and BioTechCo Inc. — each land in their own bucket. The join script normalizes case but not punctuation. Fragments that should add together never meet.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Three name variants — BioTechCo LLC, BIOTECHCO LLC, and BioTechCo Inc. — each land in their own bucket. The join script normalizes case but not punctuation. Fra

beats.name: ConsolidatedCount
beats.narration_text: Individually, each bucket looks thin — too few filings to score as a real sponsor. But enclosed together they represent the same legal filer. The true count is hidden inside fragmentation.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Individually, each bucket looks thin — too few filings to score as a real sponsor. But enclosed together they represent the same legal filer. The true count is

beats.name: CoverageAudit
beats.narration_text: The pipeline exposes this through a coverage number: what fraction of your shortlist actually matched across both datasets. If sixty-four percent matched, thirty-six percent did not. That thirty-six percent is where the artifacts live.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The pipeline exposes this through a coverage number: what fraction of your shortlist actually matched across both datasets. If sixty-four percent matched, thirt

beats.name: TwoActions
beats.narration_text: Two causes, opposite fixes. True absence: look for direct signals — a careers page, a recruiter question. Name-match artifact: resolve the entity and re-run. You cannot tell which from the tier alone. The coverage audit is what tells them apart.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two causes, opposite fixes. True absence: look for direct signals — a careers page, a recruiter question. Name-match artifact: resolve the entity and re-run. Yo

beats.name: NovaGenExample
beats.narration_text: NovaGen. Series B, three years old, sixty employees. Scores Unknown. Coverage audit: sixty-four percent of the shortlist matched. So up to thirty-six percent of Unknowns could be artifacts. Check the raw rows.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: NovaGen. Series B, three years old, sixty employees. Scores Unknown. Coverage audit: sixty-four percent of the shortlist matched. So up to thirty-six percent of

beats.name: NovaGenFix
beats.narration_text: DOL records it as NovaGen Bio LLC. USCIS records it as NOVAGEN BIO. Strip punctuation and normalize case and they match. Eight filings surface. The tier jumps to Likely. NovaGen was never a non-filer. The spelling was the problem.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: DOL records it as NovaGen Bio LLC. USCIS records it as NOVAGEN BIO. Strip punctuation and normalize case and they match. Eight filings surface. The tier jumps t

beats.name: Endcard
beats.narration_text: Unknown tier has two causes. The coverage audit tells you which one you have. Fix the join — or find the signal. Never skip both.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Unknown tier has two causes. The coverage audit tells you which one you have. Fix the join — or find the signal. Never skip both.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the The Reallocation Engine series.
